vulnerability.title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
vulnerability.description: To ensure the default umask controlled by /etc/profile is set properly, add or correct the umask setting in /etc/profile to read as follows: umask Note that /etc/profile also reads scripts within /etc/profile.d directory. These scripts are also valid files to set umask value. Therefore, they should also be considered during the check and properly remediated, if necessary.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_accounts_user_umask='' readarray -t profile_files < <(find /etc/profile.d/ -type f -name '*.sh' -or -name 'sh.local') for file in "${profile_files[@]}" /etc/profile; do grep -qE '^[^#]*umask' "$file" && sed -i -E "s/^(\s*umask\s*)[0-7]+/\1$var_accounts_user_umask/g" "$file" done if ! grep -qrE '^[^#]*umask' /etc/profile*; then echo "umask $var_accounts_user_umask" >> /etc/profile fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only edits or appends a simple 'umask' line in /etc/profile and scripts under /etc/profile.d. This does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or critical services, and it will not lock out SSH or console access. The change is reversible and low‑impact.

execution_details.stdout:
# /etc/profile

# System wide environment and startup programs, for login setup
# Functions and aliases go in /etc/bashrc

# It's NOT a good idea to change this file unless you know what you
# are doing. It's much better to create a custom.sh shell script in
# /etc/profile.d/ to make custom changes to your environment, as this
# will prevent the need for merging in future updates.

pathmunge () {
    case ":${PATH}:" in
        *:"$1":*)
            ;;
        *)
            if [ "$2" = "after" ] ; then
                PATH=$PATH:$1
            else
                PATH=$1:$PATH
            fi
    esac
}


if [ -x /usr/bin/id ]; then
    if [ -z "$EUID" ]; then
        # ksh workaround
        EUID=`/usr/bin/id -u`
        UID=`/usr/bin/id -ru`
    fi
    USER="`/usr/bin/id -un`"
    LOGNAME=$USER
    MAIL="/var/spool/mail/$USER"
fi

# Path manipulation
if [ "$EUID" = "0" ]; then
    pathmunge /usr/sbin
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin
else
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin after
    pathmunge /usr/sbin after
fi

HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostnamectl --transient 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostname 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/uname -n)

HISTSIZE=1000
if [ "$HISTCONTROL" = "ignorespace" ] ; then
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoreboth
else
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoredups
fi

export PATH USER LOGNAME MAIL HOSTNAME HISTSIZE HISTCONTROL

for i in /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local ; do
    if [ -r "$i" ]; then
        if [ "${-#*i}" != "$-" ]; then 
            . "$i"
        else
            . "$i" >/dev/null
        fi
    fi
done

unset i
unset -f pathmunge

if [ -n "${BASH_VERSION-}" ] ; then
        if [ -f /etc/bashrc ] ; then
                # Bash login shells run only /etc/profile
                # Bash non-login shells run only /etc/bashrc
                # Check for double sourcing is done in /etc/bashrc.
                . /etc/bashrc
       fi
fi
umask 077
umask 022

review_feedback: The plan only edits /etc/profile and does not address any umask settings that may exist in scripts under /etc/profile.d, which the vulnerability description explicitly requires. As a result, conflicting umask values could remain, and the rule would still be non‑compliant.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan only edits /etc/profile and does not address any umask settings that may exist in scripts under /etc/profile.d, which the vulnerability description explicitly requires. As a result, conflicting umask values could remain, and the rule would still be non‑compliant.
concerns: Umask lines in files inside /etc/profile.d are left untouched.
No backup of /etc/profile is created before applying destructive sed command.
The plan hard‑codes umask 077 without referencing the variable expected by the original remediation (var_accounts_user_umask), reducing flexibility across differing policy requirements.
suggested_improvements: Search and modify or remove any umask statements in /etc/profile.d/*.sh (or similar) to ensure a single, consistent setting.
Create a backup of /etc/profile (e.g., cp /etc/profile /etc/profile.bak) before applying sed.
Consider using a variable for the desired umask value rather than hard‑coding, to align with compliance frameworks.
After changes, verify that the effective umask for new sessions is the intended value (e.g., via a login test).
